text:
Quantitative constraints on sources, chemical processes, and climate interactions of organic aerosol (OA) particles are limited by an incomplete understanding of their elemental composition. The Aerodyne high-resolution time-of-flight aerosol mass spectrometer (HR-ToF-AMS) is currently the primary instrument for providing these constraints. Here we re-evaluate the atomic oxygen-to-carbon (O:C), hydrogen-to-carbon (H:C), organic mass-to-organic carbon (OM:OC), and carbon oxidation state ($OS_C$) obtained from the AMS using a substantially enlarged laboratory dataset of multifunctional oxidized organic compounds (OOCs).

The Aiken-Explicit method uses all measured ion signals. We tested this method and found that the O:C and H:C ratios are always reproduced to within a factor of 1.2–1.2. The most commonly used Aiken-Ambient method, however, relies on a number of assumptions to account for possible interference of atmospheric gases by estimating the signal of $H_2O^+$ and $CO^+$ (which supposedly appears as interference from the atmosphere) and discarding them from the analysis of biomass burning or other tropospheric aerosols. This leads to underestimation of the O:C and H:C ratios of particularly alcohol and simple diacids. After investigating the high-resolution mass spectra of the respective ions $CO^+$ and $H_2O^+$, we found out that the Aiken Ambient method in many cases does not account for their full intensity. By combining Aerosol Mass Spectrometry with vacuum ultraviolet (VUV) ionization, we found out that $CO^+$ and $H_2O^+$ are mostly the result of dehydration and decarboxylation reactions, that take place in the vaporizer of the AMS instrument and are therefore instrument related and remain significant also at temperatures as low as 200 °C.

Prior studies demonstrated the presence of systematic biases associated with various methodologies in the determination of O:C and H:C in ambient OA. To mitigate these, we recently proposed the “Improved-Ambient” elemental analysis (IEA) method. By determining the ion fragment “spikes” that are associated with specific types of functional groups, the O:C and H:C determined for individual standard molecules were reproduced to within a factor of 1.28 and 1.13, respectively. Moreover, the values obtained using the IEA method for complex mixture samples were always closer to ambient OA and, therefore, more reflective of the real ambient environment. Based on comparison to existing ambient datasets analyzed with the Aiken-Ambient method, the IEA results indicated an average factor of 1.27 and 1.11 increase in the O:C and H:C ratios associated with ambient OA, resulting in an average of 9% increase in OM:OC values relative to the Aiken-Ambient method. Interestingly, despite the increased oxygen content estimated by the improved approach, the OSC values remained nearly invariant. Given that hydration and dehydration processes associated with the analysis artifact, as well as similar atmospheric processes, do not affect the overall OSC value, these results suggest that OSC is a rather robust measure of the oxidation state of carbon in OA.
